q: According to Faraday's experiments, what produces an induced emf in a wire loop?
a: A change in magnetic flux through the loop.
The physical movement of the wire loop only.

q: What does Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction state about the magnitude of induced emf?
a: It is inversely proportional to the magnetic flux.
It is directly proportional to the rate of change of magnetic flux through that loop.
It is constant regardless of magnetic flux change.
It depends only on the number of turns.

q: What is the formula for the induced emf in a wire loop with N identical turns?
What does the minus sign in Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction indicate?
a: The magnitude of the induced emf.
The direction of the induced emf.
That the magnetic flux is decreasing.

q: Under what condition will there be no induced emf or induced current in a wire loop?
a: When the loop is moving perpendicular to magnetic field lines.
When the loop is not moving (or) is moving parallel to the magnetic field lines.
When the magnetic flux is changing rapidly.
When the loop has many turns.

q: What aspect of electromagnetic induction does Lenz's law provide?
a: The magnitude of the induced emf.
The rate of change of magnetic flux.
The direction of the induced emf.
The material properties of the loop.

q: Who stated the law describing the direction of induced emf in 1834?
What is the statement of Lenz's law?
a: An induced emf is always in a direction that aids the change in magnetic flux.
An induced emf is always constant.
An induced emf is always in a direction that opposes the change in the original magnetic flux that causes it.
An induced emf is always proportional to the magnetic flux.

q: Lenz's law is a good example of which fundamental principle?
When a magnet is moved towards a coil, what happens to the magnetic flux through the coil?
When a magnet's N pole is moved towards a coil, what pole must the end of the coil nearer the magnet act as to oppose the flux change?
If the N pole of a magnet is moved towards a coil, what is the direction of the induced current if viewed from the magnet, according to the right-hand rule?
What is the direction of induced current when the N pole of a magnet is moved away from a coil?
a: It depends on the coil material

q: What transformation of energy does Lenz's law demonstrate?
a: Mechanical work done against the opposing force experienced by the moving magnet or rotating coil is transformed into electrical energy.

q: What is the role of the mechanical work done against the opposing force experienced by a moving magnet or rotating coil?
a: It causes the magnetic flux to remain constant.
